game_name: Landstalker: The Treasures of King Nole

danger_signals.visual_form: Fantasy creatures (ogres, skeletons, ghosts, golems) rendered in isometric sprite form
danger_signals.meaning: Combat encounter required or avoidable threat; indicates dangerous dungeon/area
Puzzle, key acquisition, or combat required to advance; world state barrier

danger_signals.visual_form: Moving platforms, rolling boulders, environmental obstacles in dungeon layouts
danger_signals.meaning: Platforming danger; failure results in health loss or death

danger_signals.visual_form: End-of-dungeon arena with distinctive layout and enemy congregation
Dark, foreboding dungeon interiors with stone construction
danger_signals.meaning: High-danger zone; associated with powerful enemies (wizard Mir, cursed shrines)

safety_signals.visual_form: Settlement areas with NPCs, shops, and domestic architecture
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone; rest point, trading opportunity, plot exposition

safety_signals.visual_form: Characters standing in open areas, animated conversation poses
safety_signals.meaning: Quest givers, allies, information sources; non-hostile interaction space

safety_signals.visual_form: Wood nymph character always accompanying Nigel
safety_signals.meaning: Allied presence; narrative safety and direction indicator

safety_signals.visual_form: Vendor stalls and transaction spaces within towns
safety_signals.meaning: Resource acquisition zone; equipment upgrade opportunity

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Tribal settlements with distinctive bear-people architecture; leader Fara establishes tribal identity

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Curse-afflicted variant; crazed ritual behavior as visual manifestation of corruption
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Shrine structure; curse-infected visual state differentiates from Massan

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Dragon-like henchman Zak; castle knights in visual opposition during revolt sequence
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Castle court architecture; harbour with ships; lighthouse town of Ryuma under their control
Thieves' cave dungeon; kidnapping victims in crypt as visual evidence of criminal activity
Tower dungeon; wizard tower architecture; crypt leading to final confrontation
Princess character establishes kingdom presence; castle knights loyalty to kingdom

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Early game - exploration and alliance building
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Open overworld navigation; village environments accessible; NPCs standing peacefully in towns
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Nigel encounters bear tribes in neutral/positive context; optional combat; quest initiation through NPC dialogue

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ryuma lighthouse town shows signs of attack (damaged structures); Duke's castle displays authority architecture; dungeons increase in visual complexity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Thieves actively pursue Nigel and Friday; kidnapped NPCs indicate systematic threat; enemies in dungeons increase in difficulty

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Duke's castle transforms from safe haven to prison environment; tower dungeon becomes primary visual focus; Zak (dragon henchman) appears as visual representation of Duke's power
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Nigel imprisoned; knights in open revolt (visual state change); pursuit by Duke's forces intensifies

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Gumi tribespeople display corrupted/crazed visual state; shrine structure glows with corruption indicators; ritual preparation animations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Tribe attempts ritual sacrifice; aggressive posturing; Nigel breaks curse through combat/puzzle solution

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Platforming obstacles increase; monster density escalates; boss chambers feature larger, more visually distinctive enemies
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Doors require keys; puzzles mandate block-moving and switch-pressing; boss encounters gate progression until defeated

environmental_storytelling.example: Wizard's tower and crypt dungeon communicates Mir's isolation and corruption; lighthouse structure in Ryuma damaged by thieves tells story of recent attack without exposition
Kidnapped mayor and citizens in thieves' cave reveals Duke's criminal conspiracy; Fara's position as tribal leader establishes matriarchal structure in Massan
Fantasy creatures (ogres, skeletons, ghosts, golems) populate specific dungeons; curse-afflicted bear-people in Gumi shrine indicate magical corruption spreading through world
Journey westward from port town through progressively more complex dungeons to castle mirrors narrative escalation; dungeon placement along travel route creates geography-based story pacing
Friday's constant presence as wood nymph character provides visual continuity and establishes her as central to treasure-seeking narrative; her vulnerability during hostage scene communicates stakes
Lithograph of dragon functions as visual clue and plot token; Statue of Jypta's acquisition triggers main quest chain; collectible armor/weapons show progression and upgrades

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Walking, jumping, climbing ropes/ladders, sword swinging; picking up/throwing objects
Sword swing attacks; dodging enemy attacks; health depletion visible through heart UI
Block-moving, switch-pressing, platform navigation; slower deliberate movement
Accompanies Nigel throughout game; provides guidance through dialogue; holds hostage by thieves as vulnerability state
Aggressive movement toward Nigel; attack animations; avoidable or killable for gold/collectables
Distinctive larger sprite; damage animations; multi-phase combat patterns; positioned at dungeon terminus
Court reception; mission assignment; ornate castle presence
Ordering Nigel's imprisonment; commanding dragon henchman; causing knight uprising through oppression
Crazed animations; ritualistic pursuit of sacrifice victim; aggressive but breakable through puzzle/combat
Persistent but described as 'bumbling'; holding Friday hostage; kidnapping victims; cave dwelling

notes: Landstalker uses isometric perspective as primary visual language for communicating world layout and danger. Faction identity relies heavily on NPC character design (fur color for bear tribes) rather than iconographic sigils. Political conflict emerges through spatial progression—moving from peaceful tribes toward increasingly corrupt city/castle structures. The game communicates world-state primarily through environment architecture (damaged lighthouse, prison dungeon, tower) and NPC behavioral changes (knights in revolt, curse-afflicted tribes, imprisoned allies). Danger escalation is manifest through monster density increases and dungeon complexity, rather than explicit visual corruption except in curse-afflicted areas.
